messages.text: The Meshtastic GPS section from variant.h looks closer to the t1000-e one // ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ // GPS L76KB // ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ #define GPS_L76K #ifdef GPS_L76K #define GPS_TX_PIN D6 // 44 #define GPS_RX_PIN D7 // 43 #define HAS_GPS 1 #define GPS_BAUDRATE 9600 #define GPS_THREAD_INTERVAL 50 #define PIN_SERIAL1_TX GPS_TX_PIN #define PIN_SERIAL1_RX GPS_RX_PIN #define PIN_GPS_STANDBY D0 #define GPS_EN D18 // P1.05 #endif
messages.content_html: The Meshtastic GPS section from variant.h looks closer to the t1000-e one // ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ // GPS L76KB // ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ #define GPS_L76K #ifdef GPS_L76K #define GPS_TX_PIN D6 // 44 #define GPS_RX_PIN D7 // 43 #define HAS_GPS 1 #define GPS_BAUDRATE 9600 #define GPS_THREAD_INTERVAL 50 #define PIN_SERIAL1_TX GPS_TX_PIN #define PIN_SERIAL1_RX GPS_RX_PIN #define PIN_GPS_STANDBY D0 #define GPS_EN D18 // P1.05 #endif

messages.author: Ed
messages.text: That irritated me as well for a while. The idea that repeaters don't turn off isn't a bad one as you don't want an inadvertent command to make you have to go to it to turn it back on with USB. In any case, antenna change isn't that hard. Login to the repeater and turn off the repeat function. Now it will not tx and you are safe without an antenna for a bit till you get the new one on, then re enable repeat.
messages.content_html: That irritated me as well for a while. The idea that repeaters don't turn off isn't a bad one as you don't want an inadvertent command to make you have to go to it to turn it back on with USB. In any case, antenna change isn't that hard. Login to the repeater and turn off the repeat function. Now it will not tx and you are safe without an antenna for a bit till you get the new one on, then re enable repeat.

messages.author: Oscar Bravo
messages.text: I disagree with just setting the repeat to off, as someone can still asks for telemetry, or the repeater can send its advert while repeat is off and you swap the antenna. That would be bad luck (depending on how long the repeater is without an antenna) but that is not just a safe solution. You also have times where you may have to store the repeater and just don't want it to consume battery for nothing. Same for the mobile repeaters that you don't want permanently on (in a car, being able to switch them off when not in use/needed)
messages.content_html: I disagree with just setting the repeat to off, as someone can still asks for telemetry, or the repeater can send its advert while repeat is off and you swap the antenna. That would be bad luck (depending on how long the repeater is without an antenna) but that is not just a safe solution. You also have times where you may have to store the repeater and just don't want it to consume battery for nothing. Same for the mobile repeaters that you don't want permanently on (in a car, being able to switch them off when not in use/needed)
messages.reactions_html: 1
messages.reply_html:
Ed
That irritated me as well for a while. The idea that repeaters don't turn off isn't a bad one as you don't want an inadvertent command to make you have to go to it to turn it back on with USB. In any case, antenna change isn't that hard. Login to the repeater and turn off the repeat function. Now it will not tx and you are safe without an antenna for a bit till you get the new one on, then re enable repeat.

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: If you’re concerned about the radio, the combination of setting repeat off and lowering the tx power should prevent any radio damage. Remember that without the antenna attached the rx will be poor so it most likely wouldn’t receive any requests that it would respond to. Regarding power, yeah a workinng button would be nice but it’s not the end of the world to pull batteries if it’s going to be off for a while.
messages.content_html: If you’re concerned about the radio, the combination of setting repeat off and lowering the tx power should prevent any radio damage. Remember that without the antenna attached the rx will be poor so it most likely wouldn’t receive any requests that it would respond to. Regarding power, yeah a workinng button would be nice but it’s not the end of the world to pull batteries if it’s going to be off for a while.
messages.reply_html:
Oscar Bravo
I disagree with just setting the repeat to off, as someone can still asks for telemetry, or the repeater can send its advert while repeat is off and you swap the antenna. That would be bad luck (depending on how long the repeater is without an antenna) but that is not just a safe solution. You also have times where you may have to store the repeater and just don't want it to consume battery for nothing. Same for the mobile repeaters that you don't want permanently on (in a car, being able to switch them off when not in use/needed)

messages.author: Ed
messages.text: Good point adding dropping rx power during the swap ... will do both going forward. An on/off switch to save power? If it's a repeater not a companion that makes no sense, the repeater needs a continuous power source to recharge batteries. Two data points, I have a Solar P1 Pro sitting panel facing down in a bag in my car waiting to be deployed. The repeat is off so it doesn't confuse paths with other repeaters. It's been there well over a week, perhaps two weeks and still reports battery in mid 20's I'll take it out today and let it recharge LOL. So batteries on a well made repeater no problem and no need for on off switch. However my non solar car node that I use while driving I did put an on/off switch on it and this way I don't have to recharge it via usb too often This of course makes it no longer watertight so I am building a new car node box that will not have a switch. No one solution or design works everywhere but don't worry about battery life on a P1 if you store it for a long time ... months sure pull the batteries. (edited)
messages.content_html: Good point adding dropping rx power during the swap ... will do both going forward. An on/off switch to save power? If it's a repeater not a companion that makes no sense, the repeater needs a continuous power source to recharge batteries. Two data points, I have a Solar P1 Pro sitting panel facing down in a bag in my car waiting to be deployed. The repeat is off so it doesn't confuse paths with other repeaters. It's been there well over a week, perhaps two weeks and still reports battery in mid 20's I'll take it out today and let it recharge LOL. So batteries on a well made repeater no problem and no need for on off switch. However my non solar car node that I use while driving I did put an on/off switch on it and this way I don't have to recharge it via usb too often This of course makes it no longer watertight so I am building a new car node box that will not have a switch. No one solution or design works everywhere but don't worry about battery life on a P1 if you store it for a long time ... months sure pull the batteries. (edited)

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: It’s a temperature sensor built into the cpu. I wouldn’t expect miracles. Just use it to check the health of the device, see if it’s getting too hot mostly. Too cold generally won’t be a problem for the cpu.
messages.content_html: It’s a temperature sensor built into the cpu. I wouldn’t expect miracles. Just use it to check the health of the device, see if it’s getting too hot mostly. Too cold generally won’t be a problem for the cpu.
messages.reply_html:
mxda
There's a guide from the National Weather Service on how a weather station should be mounted to get an accurate temperature, of which none of those sensors would even meet the requirements

messages.author: mxda
messages.text: That's what I'm saying, it's not scientifically stable and here in Australia we've even had potential heat records not being recognised due to the measuring used (some weather stations are manual) While the sensor might be versatile and reliable, I wouldn't be using it to accurately measure temperature of the exterior. There are many factors when doing that
messages.content_html: That's what I'm saying, it's not scientifically stable and here in Australia we've even had potential heat records not being recognised due to the measuring used (some weather stations are manual) While the sensor might be versatile and reliable, I wouldn't be using it to accurately measure temperature of the exterior. There are many factors when doing that
messages.reply_html:
kri5irk
It’s a temperature sensor built into the cpu. I wouldn’t expect miracles. Just use it to check the health of the device, see if it’s getting too hot mostly. Too cold generally won’t be a problem for the cpu.

messages.author: Chris
messages.text: Why bother with telling the repeater what timezone it is in at all? Thats way more complicated than the app just knowing and displaying the timezone to the user. The device doesn’t care what timezone it’s in, the user does.
messages.content_html: Why bother with telling the repeater what timezone it is in at all? Thats way more complicated than the app just knowing and displaying the timezone to the user. The device doesn’t care what timezone it’s in, the user does.

messages.content_html: The repeater doesn’t even know the timezone as far as I know. It’s just UTC to the device. (edited)

messages.author: Chris
messages.text: if you're really worried solely about keeping time accurate on these, you can just attach a rtc clock to the i2c bus (the grove port on the seeed solar), the default addresses are polled by the repeater out of the box, seeed's own RTC clock look like it could fit fine without modification in the current space they have for a sensor. It runs off its own cr1225
messages.content_html: if you're really worried solely about keeping time accurate on these, you can just attach a rtc clock to the i2c bus (the grove port on the seeed solar), the default addresses are polled by the repeater out of the box, seeed's own RTC clock look like it could fit fine without modification in the current space they have for a sensor. It runs off its own cr1225
